summary: Stancil argues that as more data people work at data vendors, community interactions are increasingly shaped by corporate affiliation, turning peers into prospects and conversations into subtle marketing. The result is a loss of genuine, human connection in the data community, which he thinks can be restored only by centering shared personal experiences over tools and company agendas.
money_quotes: More than finding a human connection, I’m protecting a brand.
The problem isn’t that vendors are present in our space; it’s that vendors are present in us.

No culture of congeniality changes the fact that we’re trying to take money from each others’ pockets and cause genuine harm to each others’ careers; no amount of professional appreciation changes the fact that our community is one in an active and sincere war with itself.
We can still build a thriving community in this world; it just means it needs to be built on a different foundation—one that’s a little more human, and a little less vendor.
We may play for the name on the front of our jersey, but we keep the name on the back.
key_insight: The data community can only stay healthy if people deliberately separate their identities from their employers and prioritize human connection over vendor promotion.
